Deep networks naturally integrate low/mid/high- level features [50] and classiﬁers in an end-to-end multi- layer fashion, and the “levels” of features can be enriched by the number of stacked layers (depth). Recent evidence [41, 44] reveals that network depth is of crucial importance, and the leading results [41, 44, 13, 16] on the challenging ImageNet dataset [36] all exploit “very deep” [41] models, with a depth of sixteen [41] to thirty [16]. Driven by the signiﬁcance of depth, a question arises:Is learning better networks as easy as stacking more layers? An obstacle to answering this question was the notorious problem of vanishing/exploding gradients [1, 9], which hamper convergence from the beginning. This problem, however, has been largely addressed by normalized initial- ization [23, 9, 37, 13] and intermediate normalization layers [16], which enable networks with tens of layers to start con- verging for stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with back- propagation [22]. When deeper networks are able to start converging, a degradation problem has been exposed: with the network depth increasing, accuracy gets saturated (which might be unsurprising) and then degrades rapidly. Let us consider a shallower architecture and its deeper counterpart that adds more layers onto it. There exists a solution by construction to the deeper model: the added layers are identity mapping, and the other layers are copied from the learned shallower model. The existence of this constructed solution indicates that a deeper model should produce no higher training error than its shallower counterpart. reference: Deep convolutional neural networks have enabled breakthroughs in image classification by integrating features at multiple levels through many stacked layers. However, increasing the network depth introduces challenges. One major issue is the problem of vanishing/exploding gradients, which hampers convergence during training. Although techniques like normalized initialization and intermediate normalization layers help networks with many layers begin converging, a degradation problem arises: as depth increases, accuracy saturates and then degrades, which is not due to overfitting but because deeper models tend to have higher training error. This suggests that deeper networks are harder to optimize, and current solvers cannot easily find solutions better than shallower counterparts, making training deep convolutional neural networks difficult.

reference: Normalized initialization helps address the notorious problem of vanishing and exploding gradients, which hamper convergence at the start of training very deep convolutional neural networks. By properly normalizing the initial weights, it enables networks with tens of layers to begin converging when trained with stochastic gradient descent and back-propagation, thereby overcoming a key obstacle to optimizing very deep models.

user_input: What role did Microsoft Research play in advancing deep residual learning for image recognition?
reference_contexts:
['Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
Kaiming He Xiangyu Zhang Shaoqing Ren Jian Sun
Microsoft Research
{kahe, v-xiangz, v-shren, jiansun}@microsoft.com
Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8×
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNettest set. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions 1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
 added to the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short- cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa- tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas- ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19]) without modifying the solvers. We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet [36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re- sults substantially better than previous networks. Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set [20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset. We present successfully trained models on this dataset with over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers. On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152- layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on the ImageNet test set, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation competition . The extremely deep rep- resentations also have excellent generalization performance on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the 1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.']
reference: Microsoft Research developed the residual learning framework that facilitates the training of extremely deep neural networks for image recognition. Their work demonstrated that residual networks could be substantially deeper than previous models, such as VGG nets, while being easier to optimize and achieving better accuracy. The team from Microsoft Research evaluated residual networks with depths up to 152 layers, achieving a 3.57% top-5 error on the ImageNet test set and winning first place in the ILSVRC 2015 classification task. Additionally, their extremely deep residual networks led to significant improvements on other tasks, including ImageNet detection, localization, COCO detection, and segmentation, establishing the residual learning principle as a generic approach applicable to various vision problems.

reference: Kaiming He and colleagues present a residual learning framework that reformulates layers as learning residual functions with reference to layer inputs instead of unreferenced functions, which makes training extremely deep neural networks easier. Their deep residual nets are easier to optimize and gain accuracy from greatly increased depth, outperforming plain nets that exhibit higher training errors with increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset, their 152-layer residual net is much deeper than previous networks yet has lower complexity than VGG nets, achieving 3.57% top-5 error and winning 1st place in the ILSVRC 2015 classification task. Furthermore, these deep residual nets deliver a 28% relative improvement on the COCO object detection dataset and have strong generalization to other recognition tasks, leading to 1st place wins in detection, localization, and segmentation at ILSVRC and COCO 2015 competitions.

user_input: How does hierarchical basis preconditioning improve the convergence speed of solvers for Partial Differential Equations?
reference_contexts:
['2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantization,
encoding residual vectors [17] is shown to be more effec-
tive than encoding original vectors.
In low-level vision and computer graphics, for solv-
ing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), the widely used
Multigrid method [3] reformulates the system as subprob-
lems at multiple scales, where each subproblem is respon-
sible for the residual solution between a coarser and a ﬁner
scale. An alternative to Multigrid is hierarchical basis pre-
conditioning [45, 46], which relies on variables that repre-
sent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown
[3, 45, 46] that these solvers converge much faster than stan-
dard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the
solutions. These methods suggest that a good reformulation
or preconditioning can simplify the optimization.
Shortcut Connections. Practices and theories that lead to
shortcut connections [2, 34, 49] have been studied for a long
time. An early practice of training multi-layer perceptrons
(MLPs) is to add a linear layer connected from the network
input to the output [34, 49]. In [44, 24], a few interme-
diate layers are directly connected to auxiliary classiﬁers
for addressing vanishing/exploding gradients. The papers
of [39, 38, 31, 47] propose methods for centering layer re-
sponses, gradients, and propagated errors, implemented by
shortcut connections. In [44], an “inception” layer is com-
posed of a shortcut branch and a few deeper branches.
Concurrent with our work, “highway networks” [42, 43]
present shortcut connections with gating functions [15].
These gates are data-dependent and have parameters, in
contrast to our identity shortcuts that are parameter-free.
When a gated shortcut is “closed” (approaching zero), the
layers in highway networks represent non-residual func-
tions. On the contrary, our formulation always learns
residual functions; our identity shortcuts are never closed,
and all information is always passed through, with addi-
tional residual functions to be learned. In addition, high-
2

way networks have not demonstrated accuracy gains with
extremely increased depth (e.g., over 100 layers).
']
reference: Hierarchical basis preconditioning improves the convergence speed of solvers for Partial Differential Equations by relying on variables that represent residual vectors between two scales. It has been shown that these solvers converge much faster than standard solvers that are unaware of the residual nature of the solutions.

user_input: What does Fig. 2 illustrate in the context of residual learning?
reference_contexts: ['3. Deep Residual Learning 3.1. Residual Learning Let us consider H(x) as an underlying mapping to be ﬁt by a few stacked layers (not necessarily the entire net), with x denoting the inputs to the ﬁrst of these layers. If one hypothesizes that multiple nonlinear layers can asymptoti- cally approximate complicated functions 2, then it is equiv- alent to hypothesize that they can asymptotically approxi- mate the residual functions, i.e.,H(x)− x (assuming that the input and output are of the same dimensions). So rather than expect stacked layers to approximateH(x), we explicitly let these layers approximate a residual function F (x) := H(x)− x. The original function thus becomes F (x)+ x. Although both forms should be able to asymptot- ically approximate the desired functions (as hypothesized), the ease of learning might be different. This reformulation is motivated by the counterintuitive phenomena about the degradation problem (Fig. 1, left). As we discussed in the introduction, if the added layers can be constructed as identity mappings, a deeper model should have training error no greater than its shallower counter- part. The degradation problem suggests that the solvers might have difﬁculties in approximating identity mappings by multiple nonlinear layers. With the residual learning re- formulation, if identity mappings are optimal, the solvers may simply drive the weights of the multiple nonlinear lay- ers toward zero to approach identity mappings. In real cases, it is unlikely that identity mappings are op- timal, but our reformulation may help to precondition the problem. If the optimal function is closer to an identity mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the solver to ﬁnd the perturbations with reference to an identity mapping, than to learn the function as a new one. We show by experiments (Fig. 7) that the learned residual functions in general have small responses, suggesting that identity map- pings provide reasonable preconditioning. 3.2. Identity Mapping by Shortcuts We adopt residual learning to every few stacked layers. A building block is shown in Fig. 2. Formally, in this paper we consider a building block deﬁned as: y =F (x,{Wi}) + x. (1) Here x and y are the input and output vectors of the lay- ers considered. The function F (x,{Wi}) represents the residual mapping to be learned. For the example in Fig. 2 that has two layers, F = W2σ(W1x) in which σ denotes 2This hypothesis, however, is still an open question. See [28]. ReLU [29] and the biases are omitted for simplifying no- tations. The operation F + x is performed by a shortcut connection and element-wise addition. We adopt the sec- ond nonlinearity after the addition (i.e.,σ(y), see Fig. 2). The shortcut connections in Eqn.(1) introduce neither ex- tra parameter nor computation complexity. This is not only attractive in practice but also important in our comparisons between plain and residual networks. We can fairly com- pare plain/residual networks that simultaneously have the same number of parameters, depth, width, and computa- tional cost (except for the negligible element-wise addition). The dimensions of x andF must be equal in Eqn.(1). If this is not the case ( e.g., when changing the input/output channels), we can perform a linear projection Ws by the shortcut connections to match the dimensions: y =F (x,{Wi}) +Wsx. (2) We can also use a square matrixWs in Eqn.(1). But we will show by experiments that the identity mapping is sufﬁcient for addressing the degradation problem and is economical, and thusWs']
reference: Fig. 2 illustrates a building block in residual learning, where the residual mapping F(x, {Wi}) is learned and added to the input x through a shortcut connection and element-wise addition.

user_input: How is the global average pooling layer utilized in the 34-layer plain network architecture described for ImageNet, and what role does it play in the overall network design?
reference_contexts: ['is only used when matching dimensions. The form of the residual function F is ﬂexible. Exper- iments in this paper involve a function F that has two or three layers (Fig. 5), while more layers are possible. But if F has only a single layer, Eqn.(1) is similar to a linear layer: y =W1x + x, for which we have not observed advantages. We also note that although the above notations are about fully-connected layers for simplicity, they are applicable to convolutional layers. The function F (x,{Wi}) can repre- sent multiple convolutional layers. The element-wise addi- tion is performed on two feature maps, channel by channel. 3.3. Network Architectures We have tested various plain/residual nets, and have ob- served consistent phenomena. To provide instances for dis- cussion, we describe two models for ImageNet as follows. Plain Network. Our plain baselines (Fig. 3, middle) are mainly inspired by the philosophy of VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3, left). The convolutional layers mostly have 3×3 ﬁlters and follow two simple design rules: (i) for the same output feature map size, the layers have the same number of ﬁl- ters; and (ii) if the feature map size is halved, the num- ber of ﬁlters is doubled so as to preserve the time com- plexity per layer. We perform downsampling directly by convolutional layers that have a stride of 2. The network ends with a global average pooling layer and a 1000-way fully-connected layer with softmax. The total number of weighted layers is 34 in Fig. 3 (middle). It is worth noticing that our model has fewer ﬁlters and lower complexity than VGG nets [41] (Fig. 3, left). The global average pooling layer is used to aggregate the spatial dimensions of the final feature maps into a single value per channel, effectively reducing the feature map size to 1x1 before passing to the fully-connected classification layer. This design helps to maintain a lower model complexity compared to VGG nets and supports the simple structure of convolutional layers with 3x3 filters that follow the network's design rules.

reference: A Plain Network in deep learning architectures is inspired by the philosophy of VGG nets and primarily uses convolutional layers with 3×3 filters. It follows two design rules: (i) for the same output feature map size, the layers have the same number of filters; and (ii) if the feature map size is halved, the number of filters is doubled to preserve the time complexity per layer. Downsampling is performed directly by convolutional layers with a stride of 2. The network concludes with a global average pooling layer and a 1000-way fully-connected layer with softmax. The total number of weighted layers is 34, and it has fewer filters and lower complexity than VGG nets, with 3.6 billion FLOPs, which is only 18% of VGG-19's 19.6 billion FLOPs.

Example network architectures for ImageNet. Left: the VGG-19 model [41] (19.6 billion FLOPs) as a reference. Mid- dle: a plain network with 34 parameter layers (3.6 billion FLOPs). Right: a residual network with 34 parameter layers (3.6 billion FLOPs). The dotted shortcuts increase dimensions. Table 1shows more details and other variants. Residual Network. Based on the above plain network, we insert shortcut connections (Fig. 3, right) which turn the network into its counterpart residual version. The identity shortcuts (Eqn.(1)) can be directly used when the input and output are of the same dimensions (solid line shortcuts in Fig. 3). When the dimensions increase (dotted line shortcuts in Fig. 3), we consider two options: (A) The shortcut still performs identity mapping, with extra zero entries padded for increasing dimensions. This option introduces no extra parameter; (B) The projection shortcut in Eqn.(2) is used to match dimensions (done by 1×1 convolutions). For both options, when the shortcuts go across feature maps of two sizes, they are performed with a stride of 2. 3.4. Implementation Our implementation for ImageNet follows the practice in [21, 41]. The image is resized with its shorter side ran- domly sampled in [256, 480] for scale augmentation [41]. A 224×224 crop is randomly sampled from an image or its horizontal ﬂip, with the per-pixel mean subtracted [21]. The standard color augmentation in [21] is used. We adopt batch normalization (BN) [16] right after each convolution and before activation, following [16]. We initialize the weights as in [13] and train all plain/residual nets from scratch. We use SGD with a mini-batch size of 256. The learning rate starts from 0.1 and is divided by 10 when the error plateaus, and the models are trained for up to 60× 104 iterations. We use a weight decay of 0.0001 and a momentum of 0.9. We do not use dropout [14], following the practice in [16]. In testing, for comparison studies we adopt the standard 10-crop testing [21]. For best results, we adopt the fully- convolutional form as in [41, 13], and average the scores at multiple scales (images are resized such that the shorter side is in{224, 256, 384, 480, 640}).']
reference: Residual Network is based on a plain network where shortcut connections are inserted, turning it into its residual version. The identity shortcuts can be directly used when input and output have the same dimensions (solid line shortcuts). When dimensions increase (dotted line shortcuts), there are two options: (A) the shortcut still performs identity mapping with extra zero entries padded for increasing dimensions, introducing no extra parameters; (B) a projection shortcut using 1x1 convolutions is applied to match dimensions. When shortcuts go across feature maps of two sizes, they use a stride of 2. This design helps in building 34-layer residual networks matching the computational cost of plain networks while improving performance.

reference: Identity shortcuts are particularly important for the bottleneck architectures in ImageNet ResNet models because they do not increase the time complexity or model size. In the deeper bottleneck design, which uses a stack of three layers (1×1, 3×3, and 1×1 convolutions), the identity shortcut connects low-dimensional ends without introducing extra parameters. If the identity shortcut were replaced with projection shortcuts, the time complexity and model size would be doubled, as the shortcut would then be connected to the two high-dimensional ends. Therefore, using identity shortcuts leads to more efficient models for the bottleneck architectures.

On the CIFAR-10 test set with data augmentation, Highway networks and ResNets are compared in terms of classification error, depth, and number of parameters as summarized in Table 6.

For Highway networks [42, 43]:
- A 19-layer Highway network with 2.3M parameters achieves an error of 7.54% (reported as 7.54 (7.72±0.16)).
- A 32-layer Highway network with 1.25M parameters achieves an error of 8.80%.

For ResNets:
- ResNet-20 (20 layers, 0.27M parameters) has 8.75% error.
- ResNet-32 (32 layers, 0.46M parameters) has 7.51% error.
- ResNet-44 (44 layers, 0.66M parameters) has 7.17% error.
- ResNet-56 (56 layers, 0.85M parameters) has 6.97% error.
- ResNet-110 (110 layers, 1.7M parameters) has 6.43% error (reported as 6.43 (6.61±0.16) over 5 runs).
- ResNet-1202 (1202 layers, 19.4M parameters) has 7.93% error.

All methods in the table, including Highway networks and ResNets, are evaluated with data augmentation. The table shows that the 19-layer Highway network (2.3M parameters, 7.54% error) is competitive with shallower ResNets, while deeper ResNets (e.g., 56 and 110 layers) achieve lower error rates with fewer or comparable parameters than the reported Highway configurations.

When there are more layers, an individual layer of ResNets tends to modify the signal less. Exploring Over 1000 layers. We explore an aggressively deep model of over 1000 layers. We set n = 200 that leads to a 1202-layer network, which is trained as described above. Our method shows no optimization difﬁculty , and this 103-layer network is able to achieve training error <0.1% (Fig. 6, right). Its test error is still fairly good (7.93%, Table 6). But there are still open problems on such aggressively deep models. Object detection mAP (%) on the PASCAL VOC 2007/2012 test sets using baseline Faster R-CNN. Object detection mAP (%) on the COCO validation set using baseline Faster R-CNN. See also Table 9 for better results. have similar training error. We argue that this is because of overﬁtting. The 1202-layer network may be unnecessarily large (19.4M) for this small dataset. Strong regularization such as maxout [10] or dropout [14] is applied to obtain the best results ([10, 25, 24, 35]) on this dataset. In this paper, we use no maxout/dropout and just simply impose regular- ization via deep and thin architectures by design, without distracting from the focus on the difﬁculties of optimiza- tion. But combining with stronger regularization may im- prove results, which we will study in the future. 4.3. Object Detection on PASCAL and MS COCO Our method has good generalization performance on other recognition tasks. Table 7 and 8 show the object de- tection baseline results on PASCAL VOC 2007 and 2012 [5] and COCO [26]. We adoptFaster R-CNN [32] as the de- tection method. Here we are interested in the improvements of replacing VGG-16 [41] with ResNet-101. The detection implementation (see appendix) of using both models is the same, so the gains can only be attributed to better networks. Most remarkably, on the challenging COCO dataset we ob- tain a 6.0% increase in COCO’s standard metric (mAP@[.5, .95]), which is a 28% relative improvement. This gain is solely due to the learned representations. Based on deep residual nets, we won the 1st places in several tracks in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions: Im- ageNet detection, ImageNet localization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation. The details are in the appendix. 8 References [1] Y . Bengio, P. Simard, and P. Frasconi. Learning long-term dependen- cies with gradient descent is difﬁcult. IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks,']
reference: ResNet-101 shows improved performance over VGG-16 in object detection tasks, as evidenced by a higher mean average precision (mAP) on both the PASCAL VOC 2007/2012 test sets and the COCO validation set. Specifically, ResNet-101 achieves a mAP of 76.4% on VOC 2007 and 73.8% on VOC 2012, compared to VGG-16's 73.2% and 70.4%, respectively. On the COCO validation set, ResNet-101 achieves a mAP of 48.4% compared to VGG-16's 41.5%, representing a 6.0% increase in COCO's standard metric (mAP@[.5, .95]), which is a 28% relative improvement. This gain is attributed to the better learned representations of ResNet-101.

reference: The ResNet architecture contributed to winning the 1st places in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions by providing improved learned representations that enhanced visual recognition performance. Despite the challenges of optimizing deep networks, ResNet's design, which includes residual functions that are generally closer to zero than non-residual functions, allowed for deeper models without optimization difficulties. This architecture demonstrated good generalization performance on recognition tasks, as evidenced by the significant improvements in object detection metrics on datasets like PASCAL VOC and COCO. Specifically, replacing VGG-16 with ResNet-101 resulted in a 6.0% increase in COCO's standard metric (mAP@[.5, .95]), a 28% relative improvement, solely due to the learned representations. These advancements were crucial in achieving top positions in the competitions.

C. ImageNet Localization The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we assume that the image-level classiﬁers are ﬁrst adopted for predicting the class labels of an image, and the localiza- tion algorithm only accounts for predicting bounding boxes based on the predicted classes. reference: The Faster R-CNN detection method initialized with ResNet-101 improves object detection performance in the ILSVRC context by using a deeper network without hidden fully connected layers, unlike VGG-16. ResNet-101 computes full-image shared convolutional feature maps up to conv4_x, which are analogous to the 13 convolutional layers in VGG-16, maintaining the same total stride of 16 pixels. These layers are shared by both the region proposal network (RPN) and the Fast R-CNN detection network. RoI pooling is performed before conv5_1, with conv5_x layers applied on the RoI-pooled features, taking the role of VGG-16’s fully connected layers. Batch Normalization (BN) layers are fixed after pre-training on ImageNet to reduce memory consumption during fine-tuning. This architecture and feature sharing allow ResNet-101 to improve mean Average Precision (mAP) by over 3% compared to VGG-16, demonstrating enhanced feature learning and overall detection accuracy in the ILSVRC and COCO competitions.

reference_contexts: ['<1-hop>\n\na 6% increase of mAP@[.5, .95] over VGG-16, which is a 28% relative improvement, solely con- tributed by the features learned by the better network. Re- markably, the mAP@[.5, .95]’s absolute increase (6.0%) is nearly as big as mAP@.5’s (6.9%). This suggests that a deeper network can improve both recognition and localiza- tion. B. Object Detection Improvements For completeness, we report the improvements made for the competitions. These improvements are based on deep features and thus should beneﬁt from residual learning. MS COCO Box reﬁnement. Our box reﬁnement partially follows the it- erative localization in [6]. In Faster R-CNN, the ﬁnal output is a regressed box that is different from its proposal box. So for inference, we pool a new feature from the regressed box and obtain a new classiﬁcation score and a new regressed box. We combine these 300 new predictions with the orig- inal 300 predictions. Non-maximum suppression (NMS) is applied on the union set of predicted boxes using an IoU threshold of 0.3 [8], followed by box voting [6]. Box re- ﬁnement improves mAP by about 2 points (Table 9). Global context. We combine global context in the Fast R-CNN step. Given the full-image conv feature map, we pool a feature by global Spatial Pyramid Pooling [12] (with a “single-level” pyramid) which can be implemented as “RoI” pooling using the entire image’s bounding box as the RoI. This pooled feature is fed into the post-RoI layers to obtain a global context feature. This global feature is con- catenated with the original per-region feature, followed by the sibling classiﬁcation and box regression layers. This new structure is trained end-to-end. Global context im- proves mAP@.5 by about 1 point (Table 9). Multi-scale testing. In the above, all results are obtained by single-scale training/testing as in [32], where the image’s shorter side is s = 600 pixels. Multi-scale training/testing has been developed in [12, 7] by selecting a scale from a feature pyramid, and in [33] by using maxout layers. In our current implementation, we have performed multi-scale testing following [33]; we have not performed multi-scale training because of limited time. In addition, we have per- formed multi-scale testing only for the Fast R-CNN step (but not yet for the RPN step). On COCO val, the baseline Faster R-CNN with VGG-16 achieves an mAP@.5 of 41.5 and an mAP@[.5, .95] of 21.2. When switching to ResNet-101 and progressively adding box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing, the performance on COCO val improves as follows:

- Baseline Faster R-CNN (ResNet-101): mAP@.5 = 48.4, mAP@[.5, .95] = 27.2
- + box refinement: mAP@.5 = 49.9, mAP@[.5, .95] = 29.9
- + context: mAP@.5 = 51.1, mAP@[.5, .95] = 30.0
- + multi-scale testing: mAP@.5 = 53.8, mAP@[.5, .95] = 32.5

Comparing the final configuration (ResNet-101 with box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing) to the original VGG-16 baseline on COCO val, the mAP@.5 increases from 41.5 to 53.8 (a gain of 12.3 points), and the mAP@[.5, .95] increases from 21.2 to 32.5 (a gain of 11.3 points). These results indicate that using a deeper network (ResNet-101) together with box refinement, global context, and multi-scale testing substantially improves COCO val performance, enhancing both recognition and localization quality as reflected in the higher mAP@[.5, .95].

C. ImageNet Localization The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we assume that the image-level classiﬁers are ﬁrst adopted for predicting the class labels of an image, and the localiza- tion algorithm only accounts for predicting bounding boxes based on the predicted classes. We adopt the “per-class re- gression” (PCR) strategy [40, 41], learning a bounding box regressor for each class. We pre-train the networks for Im- ageNet classiﬁcation and then ﬁne-tune them for localiza- tion. We train networks on the provided 1000-class Ima- geNet training set. Our localization algorithm is based on the RPN frame- work of [32] with a few modiﬁcations. Unlike the way in [32] that is category-agnostic, our RPN for localization is designed in a per-class form. This RPN ends with two sib- ling 1×1 convolutional layers for binary classiﬁcation (cls) and box regression (reg), as in [32]. The cls and reg layers are both in a per-class from, in contrast to [32]. Speciﬁ- cally, the cls layer has a 1000-d output, and each dimension is binary logistic regression for predicting being or not be- ing an object class; the reg layer has a 1000×4-d output consisting of box regressors for 1000 classes. As in [32], our bounding box regression is with reference to multiple translation-invariant “anchor” boxes at each position. As in our ImageNet classiﬁcation training (Sec. 3.4), we randomly sample 224×224 crops for data augmentation. We use a mini-batch size of 256 images for ﬁne-tuning. To avoid negative samples being dominate, 8 anchors are ran- domly sampled for each image, where the sampled positive and negative anchors have a ratio of 1:1 [32]. For testing, the network is applied on the image fully-convolutionally. Table 13 compares the localization results. Following [41], we ﬁrst perform “oracle” testing using the ground truth class as the classiﬁcation prediction. Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
Kaiming He Xiangyu Zhang Shaoqing Ren Jian Sun
Microsoft Research
{kahe, v-xiangz, v-shren, jiansun}@microsoft.com
Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8×
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNettest set. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions 1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
 added to the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short- cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa- tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas- ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19]) without modifying the solvers. We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet [36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re- sults substantially better than previous networks. Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set [20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset. We present successfully trained models on this dataset with over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers. On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152- layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on the ImageNet test set, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation competition . The extremely deep rep- resentations also have excellent generalization performance on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the 1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.']
reference: The use of ResNet-101 significantly contributed to winning the ILSVRC 2015 detection and localization tasks by reducing localization errors and improving detection performance. In the ImageNet Localization task, ResNet-101 reduced the center-crop error to 13.3% and further to 11.7% with dense and multi-scale testing, demonstrating excellent performance compared to previous methods. Additionally, the ensemble of models using ResNet-101 achieved 62.1% mAP on the DET test set, surpassing the second place by 8.5 points. The deep residual learning framework, which allows for training networks substantially deeper than those used previously, was crucial in achieving these results, as it eased the optimization of deep networks and improved accuracy through increased depth.

reference: The ResNet architecture addresses the degradation problem seen in deeper plain networks by incorporating residual learning, which allows training of very deep networks without increasing training error. In the ImageNet classification experiments, the 34-layer plain network exhibited higher training and validation errors compared to the shallower 18-layer plain network, indicating degradation. However, ResNet-34 outperformed its plain 34-layer counterpart by achieving lower top-1 validation error (25.03% vs. 28.54%) without increasing model parameters. Training curves show that while the 34-layer plain net had consistently higher training error, the 34-layer ResNet maintained lower training and validation errors, demonstrating that residual connections effectively mitigate the degradation problem in deeper networks.

reference: GoogLeNet achieved a localization error of 26.7% on the ImageNet dataset, as reported in the ILSVRC'14 competition. This performance is compared to other models like VGG, which had a localization error of 25.3%, and the method described in the context, which achieved a significantly lower error of 8.9%. Residual learning, as implemented in ResNet models, plays a crucial role in improving deep learning models by addressing the degradation problem and allowing for deeper networks. For instance, the ResNet-34 model exhibits lower training error and better generalization compared to its plain counterpart, demonstrating the effectiveness of residual learning in enhancing model performance.

The 1202-layer network may be unnecessarily large (19.4M) for this small dataset. Strong regularization such as maxout [10] or dropout [14] is applied to obtain the best results ([10, 25, 24, 35]) on this dataset. In this paper, we use no maxout/dropout and just simply impose regular- ization via deep and thin architectures by design, without distracting from the focus on the difﬁculties of optimiza- tion. Object Detection on PASCAL and MS COCO Our method has good generalization performance on other recognition tasks. Table 7 and 8 show the object de- tection baseline results on PASCAL VOC 2007 and 2012 [5] and COCO [26]. We adoptFaster R-CNN [32] as the de- tection method. Here we are interested in the improvements of replacing VGG-16 [41] with ResNet-101. The detection implementation (see appendix) of using both models is the same, so the gains can only be attributed to better networks. Most remarkably, on the challenging COCO dataset we ob- tain a 6.0% increase in COCO’s standard metric (mAP@[.5, .95]), which is a 28% relative improvement. This gain is solely due to the learned representations. reference: ResNet-56 exhibits smaller magnitudes of responses compared to ResNet-20 and ResNet-110, indicating that individual layers in deeper ResNets tend to modify the signal less. This supports the basic motivation that residual functions might be generally closer to zero than non-residual functions. When exploring aggressively deep models like the 1202-layer network (over 1000 layers), the method shows no optimization difficulty and achieves very low training error (<0.1%). However, despite similar training error, the 1202-layer network's test error is worse than that of the 110-layer network, which is attributed to overfitting, suggesting that excessively deep networks like 1202 layers may be unnecessarily large for small datasets. This illustrates that while deeper models like ResNet-56 and beyond reduce response magnitudes layer-wise and optimize well, increasing depth beyond a point can lead to overfitting and reduced test performance.

reference: Fig. 5 is referenced in the context of illustrating the architectural differences between plain networks and ResNets. The architectures for ImageNet are detailed in Table 1, where building blocks are shown in brackets and are also depicted in Fig. 5. The architectural differences between plain networks and ResNets are significant because ResNets introduce residual connections that help mitigate the degradation problem observed in deeper networks. This is evident from the training and validation errors shown in Fig. 4, where ResNets demonstrate lower error rates compared to their plain counterparts. Specifically, the 34-layer ResNet has a lower top-1 error rate of 25.03% compared to the 34-layer plain net's 28.54%, indicating improved performance in visual recognition tasks due to the architectural enhancements provided by residual connections.

Based on the above plain network, we insert shortcut connections (Fig. 3, right) which turn the network into its counterpart residual version. The identity shortcuts (Eqn.(1)) can be directly used when the input and output are of the same dimensions (solid line shortcuts in Fig. 3). When the dimensions increase (dotted line shortcuts in Fig. 3), we consider two options: (A) The shortcut still performs identity mapping, with extra zero entries padded for increasing dimensions. This option introduces no extra parameter; (B) The projection shortcut in Eqn.(2) is used to match dimensions (done by 1×1 convolutions). For both options, when the shortcuts go across feature maps of two sizes, they are performed with a stride of 2. 3.4. Implementation Our implementation for ImageNet follows the practice in [21, 41]. The image is resized with its shorter side ran- domly sampled in [256, 480] for scale augmentation [41]. A 224×224 crop is randomly sampled from an image or its horizontal ﬂip, with the per-pixel mean subtracted [21]. The standard color augmentation in [21] is used. We adopt batch normalization (BN) [16] right after each convolution and before activation, following [16]. We initialize the weights as in [13] and train all plain/residual nets from scratch. The VGG-19 architecture consists of convolutional layers with mostly 3x3 filters, following design rules where layers with the same output feature map size have the same number of filters, and the number of filters doubles when the feature map size is halved. It ends with a global average pooling layer and a 1000-way fully-connected layer with softmax. In contrast, the 34-layer plain network has fewer filters and lower complexity, while the residual network introduces shortcut connections to the plain network, allowing identity mapping or projection shortcuts to match dimensions. Both the plain and residual networks are optimized for efficiency, maintaining lower complexity compared to VGG-19 while still being effective for visual recognition tasks.

reference: GoogLeNet achieves better top-5 error rates on ImageNet classification partly because it overcomes the optimization difficulties faced by deep plain networks, which have exponentially low convergence rates affecting training error reduction. Unlike plain networks, residual networks (ResNets) introduce shortcut connections that help address the degradation problem and allow for accuracy gains from increased depth, as seen in the improved error rates of ResNet-34 and deeper variants compared to plain nets. Moreover, these classification improvements translate into enhanced detection performance when using ResNet-101 as the backbone for object detection systems like Faster R-CNN. Specifically, ResNet-101 based detectors achieve state-of-the-art mAP scores on PASCAL VOC 2007 (85.6%), PASCAL VOC 2012 (83.8%), and the ImageNet Detection task, significantly outperforming previous benchmarks and showing how classification advances with residual learning benefit detection tasks as well.

reference: ResNets are better than plain nets for very deep networks because they effectively address the degradation problem that plain nets face during optimization. Specifically, residual learning allows the 34-layer ResNet to achieve considerably lower training error and better generalize to validation data compared to its plain counterpart, as shown by a 2.8% improvement in accuracy. Additionally, residual networks converge faster and show accuracy gains with increased depth, unlike plain networks that may have exponentially low convergence rates. Regarding comparison with state-of-the-art methods, the 152-layer ResNet achieves a single-model top-5 validation error of 4.49%, which outperforms all previous ensemble results reported before. An ensemble combining six models including two 152-layer ResNets further reduces the top-5 error on the ImageNet test set to 3.57%, winning first place in the ILSVRC 2015 competition. These performance metrics demonstrate that deeper ResNets significantly improve accuracy and benchmark results compared to plain networks and other leading architectures.

user_input: how does the identity shortcut in ResNet bottleneck architectures affect optimization ease and model complexity compared to projection shortcuts, and how does this relate to ResNet's ability to overcome optimization difficulties in deeper networks?
reference_contexts:

the net is “not overly deep” (18 layers here), the current SGD solver is still able to ﬁnd good solutions to the plain net. In this case, the ResNet eases the optimization by providing faster conver- gence at the early stage. Identity vs. Projection Shortcuts. We have shown that 3x3, 64 1x1, 64 relu 1x1, 256 relu relu 3x3, 64 3x3, 64 relu relu 64-d 256-d Figure 5. A deeper residual function F for ImageNet. Left: a building block (on 56×56 feature maps) as in Fig. 3 for ResNet- 34. Right: a “bottleneck” building block for ResNet-50/101/152. parameter-free, identity shortcuts help with training. Next we investigate projection shortcuts (Eqn.(2)). In Table 3 we compare three options: (A) zero-padding shortcuts are used for increasing dimensions, and all shortcuts are parameter- free (the same as Table 2 and Fig. 4 right); (B) projec- tion shortcuts are used for increasing dimensions, and other shortcuts are identity; and (C) all shortcuts are projections. Table 3 shows that all three options are considerably bet- ter than the plain counterpart. B is slightly better than A. We argue that this is because the zero-padded dimensions in A indeed have no residual learning. C is marginally better than B, and we attribute this to the extra parameters introduced by many (thirteen) projection shortcuts. But the small dif- ferences among A/B/C indicate that projection shortcuts are not essential for addressing the degradation problem. So we do not use option C in the rest of this paper, to reduce mem- ory/time complexity and model sizes. reference: In ResNet bottleneck architectures, identity shortcuts are particularly important because they do not increase the time complexity or model size, unlike projection shortcuts. Specifically, replacing the identity shortcut with a projection doubles both time complexity and model size since the shortcut connects high-dimensional ends. The identity shortcuts thus enable more efficient models, reducing memory and time complexity while maintaining training effectiveness. This design choice helps ease optimization by providing faster convergence at the early stages, even for nets that are not overly deep (such as an 18-layer net). Additionally, while plain deep networks suffer from increased training error as depth increases due to fundamental optimization difficulties, ResNets, which employ identity shortcuts in their residual blocks, overcome these difficulties, demonstrating improved accuracy gains with increased depth. This is evidenced by experiments on CIFAR-10 where ResNets show consistent convergence and lower errors even at depths like 110 layers. Therefore, the use of identity shortcuts in ResNet bottleneck designs both reduces complexity and improves optimization, enabling very deep networks to be effectively trained.

The above results are only based on theproposal network (RPN) in Faster R-CNN [32]. One may use the detection network (Fast R-CNN [7]) in Faster R-CNN to improve the results. But we notice that on this dataset, one image usually contains a single dominate object, and the proposal regions highly overlap with each other and thus have very similar RoI-pooled features. As a result, the image-centric training of Fast R-CNN [7] generates samples of small variations, which may not be desired for stochastic training. Motivated by this, in our current experiment we use the original R- CNN [8] that is RoI-centric, in place of Fast R-CNN. Our R-CNN implementation is as follows. We apply the per-class RPN trained as above on the training images to predict bounding boxes for the ground truth class. These predicted boxes play a role of class-dependent proposals. For each training image, the highest scored 200 proposals are extracted as training samples to train an R-CNN classi- ﬁer. The image region is cropped from a proposal, warped to 224×224 pixels, and fed into the classiﬁcation network as in R-CNN [8]. The outputs of this network consist of two sibling fc layers for cls and reg, also in a per-class form. This R-CNN network is ﬁne-tuned on the training set us- ing a mini-batch size of 256 in the RoI-centric fashion. For testing, the RPN generates the highest scored 200 proposals for each predicted class, and the R-CNN network is used to update these proposals’ scores and box positions. This method reduces the top-5 localization error to 10.6% (Table 13). This is our single-model result on the validation set. Using an ensemble of networks for both clas- siﬁcation and localization, we achieve a top-5 localization error of 9.0% on the test set. This number signiﬁcantly out- performs the ILSVRC 14 results (Table 14), showing a 64% relative reduction of error. This result won the 1st place in the ImageNet localization task in ILSVRC 2015. 12']
reference: The use of R-CNN and ensemble networks contributed to winning the ILSVRC 2015 localization task by significantly reducing the top-5 localization error. The R-CNN implementation involved using a RoI-centric approach, where the highest scored 200 proposals were extracted as training samples to train an R-CNN classifier. This method reduced the top-5 localization error to 10.6% on the validation set. Furthermore, using an ensemble of networks for both classification and localization further reduced the error to 9.0% on the test set, outperforming the ILSVRC 14 results with a 64% relative reduction of error, ultimately securing 1st place in the ImageNet localization task.

Re- markably, the mAP@[.5, .95]’s absolute increase (6.0%) is nearly as big as mAP@.5’s (6.9%). This suggests that a deeper network can improve both recognition and localiza- tion. B. Object Detection Improvements For completeness, we report the improvements made for the competitions. These improvements are based on deep features and thus should beneﬁt from residual learning. MS COCO Box reﬁnement. Our box reﬁnement partially follows the it- erative localization in [6]. In Faster R-CNN, the ﬁnal output is a regressed box that is different from its proposal box. So for inference, we pool a new feature from the regressed box and obtain a new classiﬁcation score and a new regressed box. We combine these 300 new predictions with the orig- inal 300 predictions. Non-maximum suppression (NMS) is applied on the union set of predicted boxes using an IoU threshold of 0.3 [8], followed by box voting [6]. Box re- ﬁnement improves mAP by about 2 points (Table 9). Global context. We combine global context in the Fast R-CNN step. Given the full-image conv feature map, we pool a feature by global Spatial Pyramid Pooling [12] (with a “single-level” pyramid) which can be implemented as “RoI” pooling using the entire image’s bounding box as the RoI. This pooled feature is fed into the post-RoI layers to obtain a global context feature. This global feature is con- catenated with the original per-region feature, followed by the sibling classiﬁcation and box regression layers. This new structure is trained end-to-end. Global context im- proves mAP@.5 by about 1 point (Table 9). Multi-scale testing. In the above, all results are obtained by single-scale training/testing as in [32], where the image’s shorter side is s = 600 pixels. Multi-scale training/testing has been developed in [12, 7] by selecting a scale from a feature pyramid, and in [33] by using maxout layers. In our current implementation, we have performed multi-scale testing following [33]; we have not performed multi-scale training because of limited time. In addition, we have per- formed multi-scale testing only for the Fast R-CNN step (but not yet for the RPN step). reference: The object detection performance improvements on the MS COCO dataset demonstrate the advantages of deep residual networks through multiple factors. First, replacing VGG-16 with ResNet-101 yields a 6.0% absolute increase in mAP@[.5, .95], which corresponds to a 28% relative improvement solely due to the better learned representations of the deeper network. This suggests that deeper residual networks improve both recognition and localization capabilities. Moreover, additional refinements such as box refinement improve mAP by about 2 points by combining new predictions with original proposals followed by non-maximum suppression and box voting. Incorporating global context through pooling the entire image’s convolutional feature map and concatenating it with per-region features further improves mAP@.5 by around 1 point. Multi-scale testing, involving computation of convolutional feature maps on an image pyramid with varying shorter side lengths, raises mAP@[.5, .95] and mAP@.5 even further. These techniques together underline how deep residual networks, exemplified by ResNet-101, combined with advanced detection strategies, significantly enhance object detection results on the challenging MS COCO benchmark.

Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
Kaiming He Xiangyu Zhang Shaoqing Ren Jian Sun
Microsoft Research
{kahe, v-xiangz, v-shren, jiansun}@microsoft.com
Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8×
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNettest set. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions 1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
 added to the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short- cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa- tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas- ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19]) without modifying the solvers. We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet [36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re- sults substantially better than previous networks. Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set [20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset. We present successfully trained models on this dataset with over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers. On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152- layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on the ImageNet test set, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation competition . The extremely deep rep- resentations also have excellent generalization performance on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the 1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.']
reference: The architectures of VGG nets and deep residual networks differ significantly in terms of complexity and performance on visual recognition tasks. VGG nets, specifically VGG-19, have a high computational complexity with 19.6 billion FLOPs (multiply-adds), whereas the 34-layer plain network inspired by VGG nets has only 3.6 billion FLOPs, which is 18% of VGG-19's complexity. In contrast, deep residual networks, which can have a depth of up to 152 layers, are designed to be easier to optimize and can achieve higher accuracy due to their increased depth. Despite being 8 times deeper than VGG nets, these residual networks maintain lower complexity. On the ImageNet dataset, an ensemble of residual nets achieved a 3.57% error rate, winning the 1st place in the ILSVRC 2015 classification task. Additionally, the deep residual networks demonstrated excellent generalization performance, securing 1st places in various tasks such as ImageNet detection, localization, COCO detection, and segmentation in the ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions. This indicates that while VGG nets are computationally intensive, deep residual networks offer superior performance and efficiency in visual recognition tasks.

This approach led to a 3.57% error on the ImageNet test set, securing 1st place in the classification task. Additionally, the residual learning framework proved to be generic, providing excellent generalization performance across various recognition tasks, including ImageNet detection, localization, COCO detection, and segmentation, where it also won 1st places. In the localization task, the R-CNN implementation played a crucial role by using a RoI-centric approach instead of Fast R-CNN, which was less effective due to highly overlapping proposal regions. The R-CNN method involved predicting bounding boxes for ground truth classes using the per-class RPN, extracting the highest scored 200 proposals as training samples, and fine-tuning the network on these samples. This approach reduced the top-5 localization error to 10.6% on the validation set and further to 9.0% on the test set using an ensemble of networks, achieving a 64% relative reduction of error compared to ILSVRC 2014 results and winning 1st place in the localization task.

C. ImageNet Localization The ImageNet Localization (LOC) task [36] requires to classify and localize the objects. Following [40, 41], we assume that the image-level classiﬁers are ﬁrst adopted for predicting the class labels of an image, and the localiza- tion algorithm only accounts for predicting bounding boxes based on the predicted classes. We adopt the “per-class re- gression” (PCR) strategy [40, 41], learning a bounding box regressor for each class. We pre-train the networks for Im- ageNet classiﬁcation and then ﬁne-tune them for localiza- tion. We train networks on the provided 1000-class Ima- geNet training set. Our localization algorithm is based on the RPN frame- work of [32] with a few modiﬁcations. Unlike the way in [32] that is category-agnostic, our RPN for localization is designed in a per-class form. This RPN ends with two sib- ling 1×1 convolutional layers for binary classiﬁcation (cls) and box regression (reg), as in [32]. The cls and reg layers are both in a per-class from, in contrast to [32]. Speciﬁ- cally, the cls layer has a 1000-d output, and each dimension is binary logistic regression for predicting being or not be- ing an object class; the reg layer has a 1000×4-d output consisting of box regressors for 1000 classes. As in [32], our bounding box regression is with reference to multiple translation-invariant “anchor” boxes at each position. As in our ImageNet classiﬁcation training (Sec. 3.4), we randomly sample 224×224 crops for data augmentation. We use a mini-batch size of 256 images for ﬁne-tuning. To avoid negative samples being dominate, 8 anchors are ran- domly sampled for each image, where the sampled positive and negative anchors have a ratio of 1:1 [32]. For testing, the network is applied on the image fully-convolutionally. Table 13 compares the localization results. Following [41], we ﬁrst perform “oracle” testing using the ground truth class as the classiﬁcation prediction. reference: The RPN framework contributes to reducing localization error in the ImageNet task by implementing a per-class form of the RPN, which ends with two sibling 1×1 convolutional layers for binary classification and box regression. This approach allows for a more precise prediction of bounding boxes for each class, significantly reducing the center-crop error to 13.3% compared to VGG's 33.1%. Additionally, using dense and multi-scale testing further reduces the error to 11.7%. The RPN framework, when combined with R-CNN, reduces the top-5 localization error to 10.6% on the validation set and 9.0% on the test set, showcasing a 64% relative reduction of error compared to previous methods.

Residual Learning Let us consider H(x) as an underlying mapping to be ﬁt by a few stacked layers (not necessarily the entire net), with x denoting the inputs to the ﬁrst of these layers. If one hypothesizes that multiple nonlinear layers can asymptoti- cally approximate complicated functions 2, then it is equiv- alent to hypothesize that they can asymptotically approxi- mate the residual functions, i.e.,H(x)− x (assuming that the input and output are of the same dimensions). So rather than expect stacked layers to approximateH(x), we explicitly let these layers approximate a residual function F (x) := H(x)− x. The original function thus becomes F (x)+ x. Although both forms should be able to asymptot- ically approximate the desired functions (as hypothesized), the ease of learning might be different. This reformulation is motivated by the counterintuitive phenomena about the degradation problem (Fig. 1, left). As we discussed in the introduction, if the added layers can be constructed as identity mappings, a deeper model should have training error no greater than its shallower counter- part. The degradation problem suggests that the solvers might have difﬁculties in approximating identity mappings by multiple nonlinear layers. With the residual learning re- formulation, if identity mappings are optimal, the solvers may simply drive the weights of the multiple nonlinear lay- ers toward zero to approach identity mappings. In real cases, it is unlikely that identity mappings are op- timal, but our reformulation may help to precondition the problem. If the optimal function is closer to an identity mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the solver to ﬁnd the perturbations with reference to an identity mapping, than to learn the function as a new one. We show by experiments (Fig. 7) that the learned residual functions in general have small responses, suggesting that identity map- pings provide reasonable preconditioning. 3.2. Identity Mapping by Shortcuts We adopt residual learning to every few stacked layers. A building block is shown in Fig. 2. This is not only attractive in practice but also important in our comparisons between plain and residual networks. The residual learning reformulation helps in preconditioning the problem, which is crucial because if the optimal function is closer to an identity mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the solver to approximate it. reference: ResNets achieve superior visual recognition performance compared to other models primarily due to their increased depth, which allows for significant accuracy gains without encountering the degradation problem. The 50/101/152-layer ResNets are notably more accurate than the 34-layer ones, as demonstrated by their competitive accuracy and lower top-5 validation error rates. For instance, the 152-layer ResNet achieves a single-model top-5 validation error of 4.49%, outperforming previous ensemble results. Additionally, when combined into an ensemble, these models achieve a top-5 error of 3.57% on the test set, securing 1st place in ILSVRC 2015. On the CIFAR-10 dataset, ResNets demonstrate lower classification errors compared to other methods, with ResNet-110 achieving a classification error of 6.43%. The depth of ResNets, facilitated by shortcut connections, plays a crucial role in enhancing visual recognition performance by allowing the network to learn more complex features without increasing the number of parameters significantly.

A. Object Detection Baselines In this section we introduce our detection method based on the baseline Faster R-CNN [32] system. The models are initialized by the ImageNet classiﬁcation models, and then ﬁne-tuned on the object detection data. We have experi- mented with ResNet-50/101 at the time of the ILSVRC & COCO 2015 detection competitions. Unlike VGG-16 used in [32], our ResNet has no hidden fc layers. We adopt the idea of “Networks on Conv fea- ture maps” (NoC) [33] to address this issue. We compute the full-image shared conv feature maps using those lay- ers whose strides on the image are no greater than 16 pixels (i.e., conv1, conv2 x, conv3 x, and conv4 x, totally 91 conv layers in ResNet-101; Table 1). We consider these layers as analogous to the 13 conv layers in VGG-16, and by doing so, both ResNet and VGG-16 have conv feature maps of the same total stride (16 pixels). These layers are shared by a region proposal network (RPN, generating 300 proposals) [32] and a Fast R-CNN detection network [7]. RoI pool- ing [7] is performed before conv5 1. On this RoI-pooled feature, all layers of conv5 x and up are adopted for each region, playing the roles of VGG-16’s fc layers. The ﬁnal classiﬁcation layer is replaced by two sibling layers (classi- ﬁcation and box regression [7]). For the usage of BN layers, after pre-training, we com- pute the BN statistics (means and variances) for each layer on the ImageNet training set. Then the BN layers are ﬁxed during ﬁne-tuning for object detection. As such, the BN layers become linear activations with constant offsets and scales, and BN statistics are not updated by ﬁne-tuning. We ﬁx the BN layers mainly for reducing memory consumption in Faster R-CNN training. PASCAL VOC Following [7, 32], for the PASCAL VOC 2007 test set, we use the 5k trainval images in VOC 2007 and 16k train- val images in VOC 2012 for training (“07+12”). For the PASCAL VOC 2012 test set, we use the 10k trainval+test images in VOC 2007 and 16ktrainval images in VOC 2012 for training (“07++12”). The hyper-parameters for train- ing Faster R-CNN are the same as in [32]. Table 7 shows the results. ResNet-101 improves the mAP by >3% over VGG-16. This gain is solely because of the improved fea- tures learned by ResNet. MS COCO The MS COCO dataset [26] involves 80 object cate- gories. We evaluate the PASCAL VOC metric (mAP @ IoU = 0.5) and the standard COCO metric (mAP @ IoU = .5:.05:.95). We use the 80k images on the train set for train- ing and the 40k images on the val set for evaluation. Our detection system for COCO is similar to that for PASCAL VOC. We train the COCO models with an 8-GPU imple- mentation, and thus the RPN step has a mini-batch size of 8 images (i.e., 1 per GPU) and the Fast R-CNN step has a mini-batch size of 16 images. The RPN step and Fast R- CNN step are both trained for 240k iterations with a learn- ing rate of 0.001 and then for 80k iterations with 0.0001. Table 8 shows the results on the MS COCO validation set. ResNet-101 has', '<2-hop>

a 6% increase of mAP@[.5, .95] over VGG-16, which is a 28% relative improvement, solely con- tributed by the features learned by the better network. Re- markably, the mAP@[.5, .95]’s absolute increase (6.0%) is nearly as big as mAP@.5’s (6.9%). This suggests that a deeper network can improve both recognition and localiza- tion. B. Object Detection Improvements For completeness, we report the improvements made for the competitions. These improvements are based on deep features and thus should beneﬁt from residual learning. MS COCO Box reﬁnement. Our box reﬁnement partially follows the it- erative localization in [6]. In Faster R-CNN, the ﬁnal output is a regressed box that is different from its proposal box. So for inference, we pool a new feature from the regressed box and obtain a new classiﬁcation score and a new regressed box. We combine these 300 new predictions with the orig- inal 300 predictions. Non-maximum suppression (NMS) is applied on the union set of predicted boxes using an IoU threshold of 0.3 [8], followed by box voting [6]. Box re- ﬁnement improves mAP by about 2 points (Table 9). Global context. We combine global context in the Fast R-CNN step. Given the full-image conv feature map, we pool a feature by global Spatial Pyramid Pooling [12] (with a “single-level” pyramid) which can be implemented as “RoI” pooling using the entire image’s bounding box as the RoI. This pooled feature is fed into the post-RoI layers to obtain a global context feature. This global feature is con- catenated with the original per-region feature, followed by the sibling classiﬁcation and box regression layers. This new structure is trained end-to-end. Global context im- proves mAP@.5 by about 1 point (Table 9). Multi-scale testing. In the above, all results are obtained by single-scale training/testing as in [32], where the image’s shorter side is s = 600 pixels. Multi-scale training/testing has been developed in [12, 7] by selecting a scale from a feature pyramid, and in [33] by using maxout layers. In our current implementation, we have performed multi-scale testing following [33]; we have not performed multi-scale training because of limited time. In addition, we have per- formed multi-scale testing only for the Fast R-CNN step (but not yet for the RPN step). reference: The MS COCO dataset contributes significantly to the improvements in object detection performance using Faster R-CNN and ResNet-101 by providing a diverse set of 80 object categories for evaluation. The dataset allows for the application of advanced techniques such as box refinement, global context integration, and multi-scale testing, which collectively enhance the mean Average Precision (mAP) scores. Specifically, ResNet-101 shows a 6% increase in mAP@[.5, .95] over VGG-16, indicating a 28% relative improvement due to the superior features learned by the deeper network. Additionally, the use of global context and multi-scale testing further boosts the mAP scores, demonstrating the dataset's role in facilitating the development and optimization of deep learning models for enhanced visual recognition performance.

reference: The use of R-CNN and ensemble methods significantly contributed to the success in the ILSVRC 2015 localization task and the COCO 2015 detection task. In the ILSVRC 2015 localization task, the R-CNN implementation involved applying a per-class Region Proposal Network (RPN) to predict bounding boxes for the ground truth class, extracting the highest scored 200 proposals as training samples, and using a classification network with two sibling fully connected layers for classification and regression. This approach reduced the top-5 localization error to 10.6% on the validation set, and further using an ensemble of networks for both classification and localization achieved a top-5 localization error of 9.0% on the test set, outperforming the ILSVRC 14 results by a 64% relative reduction of error, winning 1st place in the task. In the COCO 2015 detection task, the Faster R-CNN system was enhanced with box refinement, context, and multi-scale testing, and an ensemble was used to boost both region proposal and object classification tasks. The ensemble of networks improved the mean Average Precision (mAP) to 59.0% and 37.4% on the test-dev set, securing 1st place in the detection task.

user_input: What innovations in the deep residual learning framework contributed to its success in achieving first place at ILSVRC 2015, and how did the network depth relate to performance improvements on ImageNet and other visual recognition tasks?
reference_contexts:
['<1-hop>

Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
Kaiming He Xiangyu Zhang Shaoqing Ren Jian Sun
Microsoft Research
{kahe, v-xiangz, v-shren, jiansun}@microsoft.com
Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8×
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNettest set. This result won the 1st place on the
ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation task. We also present analysis
on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers.
The depth of representations is of central importance
for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our ex-
tremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative im-
provement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep
residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC
& COCO 2015 competitions 1, where we also won the 1st
places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet local-
ization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
 added to the outputs of the stacked layers (Fig. 2). Identity short- cut connections add neither extra parameter nor computa- tional complexity. The entire network can still be trained end-to-end by SGD with backpropagation, and can be eas- ily implemented using common libraries ( e.g., Caffe [19]) without modifying the solvers. We present comprehensive experiments on ImageNet [36] to show the degradation problem and evaluate our method. We show that: 1) Our extremely deep residual nets are easy to optimize, but the counterpart “plain” nets (that simply stack layers) exhibit higher training error when the depth increases; 2) Our deep residual nets can easily enjoy accuracy gains from greatly increased depth, producing re- sults substantially better than previous networks. Similar phenomena are also shown on the CIFAR-10 set [20], suggesting that the optimization difﬁculties and the effects of our method are not just akin to a particular dataset. We present successfully trained models on this dataset with over 100 layers, and explore models with over 1000 layers. On the ImageNet classiﬁcation dataset [36], we obtain excellent results by extremely deep residual nets. Our 152- layer residual net is the deepest network ever presented on ImageNet, while still having lower complexity than VGG nets [41]. Our ensemble has 3.57% top-5 error on the ImageNet test set, and won the 1st place in the ILSVRC 2015 classiﬁcation competition . The extremely deep rep- resentations also have excellent generalization performance on other recognition tasks, and lead us to further win the 1st places on: ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions. This strong evidence shows that the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.']
reference: The deep residual learning framework introduced by Kaiming He and colleagues addressed the difficulty of training very deep neural networks by reformulating layers to learn residual functions with reference to layer inputs, instead of unreferenced functions. This approach allowed networks to be substantially deeper—up to 152 layers—while remaining easier to optimize than plain networks which suffered from higher training error as depth increased. The residual networks required no extra parameters or computational complexity because identity shortcut connections were added, enabling end-to-end training with standard SGD and backpropagation methods. On the ImageNet classification dataset, extremely deep residual nets achieved excellent results, including a 3.57% top-5 error on the test set. This ensemble outcome won first place in the ILSVRC 2015 classification competition. Moreover, the deep residual representations also generalized well to other recognition tasks, resulting in first-place wins in ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation during the ILSVRC and COCO 2015 competitions. The increase in network depth directly correlated with improved accuracy and generalization, confirming that deeper residual networks could be successfully trained and provide substantial performance gains.
